message:
fix: three small audit-pass safety fixes (cargo child cleanup, poisoned lock, malformed reclaim token)

Three independent one-site hardenings from the same audit sweep, too
small to justify a commit each.

1. rch-common/src/cargo_path_deps.rs — kill & reap child on try_wait err

   `invoke_cargo_metadata` polls a spawned `cargo metadata` subprocess
   with `child.try_wait()` in a loop. The timeout branch already
   kills+waits the child (committed in 0c98d12). The error branch —
   when `try_wait()` itself returns `Err(_)` (rare but possible: EINTR,
   EINVAL from the kernel on corrupted state) — dropped straight to a
   `return Err(...)` without touching the child at all, leaking the
   subprocess to the caller as an orphan.

   Fix: best-effort `child.kill(); child.wait();` before returning the
   error, mirroring the timeout branch's pattern. The kills are
   ignored-on-failure because there's no sensible recovery from a
   kill-after-try_wait-error anyway — the goal is just "don't leak".

   Also reflows one format! call that clippy flagged in the pre-existing
   timeout branch (three-line `.with_diagnostic(format!(\n ...))` -> one
   line) — unrelated to the fix but fell under the same rustfmt pass.

2. rchd/src/history.rs — `queued_build` uses poisoned-lock recovery

   The other readers of `self.queued` in BuildHistory use the project's
   standard pattern `.read().unwrap_or_else(|e| e.into_inner())` so that
   a prior panic under the write lock doesn't escalate to a panic in a
   harmless read query. `queued_build` was the one site that still said
   `.read().unwrap()`, which would panic-propagate on a poisoned lock.
   Harmonized with the rest of the file.

   Not a "poison is fine" statement — if the write side truly corrupted
   the Vec, readers will see that corruption. But reads here are
   `.iter().find(...).cloned()` on QueuedBuildState which is a plain
   struct; a corrupted clone is almost always preferable to a daemon
   crash during a query.

3. rchd/src/reclaim.rs — skip malformed tokens instead of aborting the line

   `parse_reclaim_metrics` splits a metrics line into
   `key=value` tokens. It used `?` on `split_once('=')`, which means a
   single token that doesn't contain `=` causes the whole line's metrics
   to be dropped (returns None from the function). Real-world output has
   shown trailing shell-produced tokens like bare `--` or a stray
   terminator that the previous behaviour turned into a silent metrics
   loss.

   Fix: `let-else continue` on the split result, so malformed tokens are
   skipped and the well-formed `key=value` pairs in the same line are
   still parsed. Any genuinely critical field absence is still caught
   later by the per-field `removed`/`dry_removed`/etc. None checks.

All three are tightenings that do not change the happy path, only the
"something unexpected happened" path.

files.patch:
@@ -598,9 +598,12 @@ impl BuildHistory {
 
     /// Get a specific queued build by ID.
     pub fn queued_build(&self, queue_id: u64) -> Option<QueuedBuildState> {
+        // Use the same poisoned-lock recovery pattern as the rest of this
+        // file: a prior panic under this lock shouldn't propagate a panic
+        // into a simple read-only query.
         self.queued
             .read()
-            .unwrap()
+            .unwrap_or_else(|e| e.into_inner())
             .iter()
             .find(|b| b.id == queue_id)
             .cloned()
